Burmese cold noodle salad with prawns

romanized: ပဲကြာဇံပုဇွန်ထောင်း

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese rice noodle prawn salad. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodle dishes) is the closest parent. No specific Burmese noodle salad code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Burmese noodle salad dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Burmese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked rice noodles, cooked prawns, and typical Burmese salad dressing ingredients, weighted to a representative recipe proportion.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese rice noodle prawn salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries: cooked rice noodles (~55% by weight), cooked prawns (~25%), shallots, garlic, lime juice, fish sauce, dried shrimp, and chilli (~20% combined). Recipe proportions are based on standard Burmese htaung (salad) preparation conventions. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles (cooked) GI approximately 53 (low); analogous estimate applied to rice noodle prawn salad dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese rice noodle prawn salad. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source (~55% by weight). The addition of prawns, lime juice, fish sauce, and vegetables is expected to lower the effective GI slightly due to protein and acid content; GI 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (53 × 19.5 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 31.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving is moderate-to-high for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control to half a plate (~150 g) and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1260 mg per serving) and moderate cholesterol from prawns (~204 mg per serving) warrant caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing smaller portions and reducing fish sauce can improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice noodle and prawn salad (ပဲကြာဇံပုဇွန်ထောင်း, romanized: Pe Kyar Zan Pazun Htaung) is a traditional Burmese cold noodle salad dish featuring thin rice vermicelli noodles and cooked prawns dressed with a pungent, tangy sauce of fish sauce, lime juice, dried shrimp, shallots, garlic, and fresh chilli. The dish is assembled cold or at room temperature, giving it a refreshing quality typical of Burmese htaung (salad) preparations. The noodles provide a soft, slightly chewy texture while the prawns contribute a firm, succulent bite. The dressing is characteristically sour, salty, and mildly spicy, with umami depth from fish sauce and dried shrimp. In Myanmar, this dish is commonly eaten as a light meal or snack, and it appears on the menus of Burmese restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy food providing approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from prawns (~7.2 g per 100 g) and a moderate carbohydrate load from rice noodles (~19.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category), though the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at approximately 31. The dish is notably high in sodium due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets. Clinically, portion control and reduced fish sauce are the primary dietary modifications recommended for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese rice noodle prawn salad exists in any approved primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component analysis using representative recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future approved regional database entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence across all component entries used for this estimation. 5. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed zero as rice noodles and prawns contain no industrially produced trans fats, and no hydrogenated oils are used in traditional Burmese htaung preparation. 6. GI of 53 is a conservative analogous estimate from cooked rice noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of the dressed salad may be lower due to acid and protein effects. 7. The cuisine tag 'Lor Mee' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform categorisation error; this dish is not Lor Mee. The tag has been disregarded. 8. Halal status is assessed as likely true given the ingredients, but formal halal certification of the specific restaurant has not been verified. 9. The food_id GD002658 is preserved from the input seed record.
